text: This volume examines educational inequalities among the children of Turkish immigrants in Austria, France, and Sweden. Despite being one of the largest immigrant populations in these countries, Turkish families frequently contend with discrimination and restricted pathways to advancement—challenges that are clearly reflected in their children’s educational experiences. Drawing on extensive cross-national data, this study explores how different school systems and family dynamics shape the academic trajectories of the second generation. A primary finding is that national education systems offering more flexible institutional arrangements—such as late tracking and higher permeability—significantly foster greater upward social and economic mobility. By highlighting the interaction between state policy and individual resources, this research provides critical insights into why some integration contexts are more successful than others in bridging the opportunity gap.
